origin: A line from the Hebrew Bible (Tanakh), commonly identified as Isaiah 53:7, part of the “Suffering Servant” passage describing silent endurance under oppression.
author: Traditionally associated with the Book of Isaiah; more specifically, scholars often place Isaiah 53 in the later layer of Isaiah (often called Deutero-Isaiah), suggesting an anonymous prophet/scribe from the Babylonian exile period rather than the historical Isaiah son of Amoz.
thoughts: The authorial perspective emphasizes paradoxical strength through silence: despite injustice and being led to destruction, the subject does not protest. The motivation appears devotional/interpretive—portraying suffering as purposeful (e.g., for redemption or divine plan) and encouraging the audience to trust in God despite persecution.
audience: First intended for an ancient Israelite/Judean community engaged in interpreting national suffering and prophetic hope (later also widely adopted by Christian audiences as messianic prophecy).
written_when: Most likely during the Babylonian exile or shortly after—commonly placed around the 6th century BCE (roughly mid–late 500s BCE), even though the text is inside the broader Isaiah tradition.
